title: Chapter 25: Silver and Black
content: The twins were born on the first full moon of spring.

Sera had been in labor for six hours — long enough that Kael had worn a groove in the stone floor of the corridor outside the healer's chamber, long enough that Fenris had composed approximately forty-seven panic poems, long enough that Darius had threatened to sedate the Alpha if he didn't stop growling at everyone who walked past.
And then, at midnight, the cries began.
Two voices. Two sets of lungs. Two tiny wolves announcing their arrival to the world.

Kael burst through the door before the healer could stop him. Sera was propped against a mountain of pillows, exhausted and glowing and more beautiful than he'd ever seen her. In her arms were two infants — one with a tuft of silver hair, one with a tuft of black.
"They're perfect," she said, her voice cracking with exhaustion and joy. "They're absolutely perfect."

Kael approached the bed like a man walking through a dream. He knelt beside Sera, his golden eyes fixed on the tiny faces of his children. The silver-haired pup — a girl, the healer murmured — opened her eyes. They were grey. Mercury-grey. Moon-silver, just like her mother's.
The black-haired pup — a boy — opened his eyes a moment later. Gold. Molten gold. Just like his father's.
"They have our eyes," Kael whispered.

"They have our wolves, too. Magnus already checked. The girl — she's Moon-Marked. Like me. She'll have the Silver Fire someday." Sera looked up at him. "And the boy — he has your Alpha power. Magnus says he's never felt a presence that strong in a newborn."
"Our children are already terrifying."
"Just like their parents."

Kael reached out — hesitantly, as if he was afraid his battle-scarred hands might break something so small — and touched his daughter's cheek. The silver-haired pup made a small, contented sound and grabbed his finger with surprising strength.
"She has your grip," he said.
"She has your stubbornness. I can already tell."

*"THEY'RE SO SMALL. SO TINY. SO PERFECT. I'M GOING TO PROTECT THEM FOREVER. NO ONE WILL EVER HURT THEM. NO SHADOWS. NO CURSES. NO ROGUES. THEY'RE GOING TO GROW UP SAFE AND HAPPY AND THEY'RE GOING TO BE THE BEST WOLVES IN THE HISTORY OF WOLVES. I'M GOING TO — okay, I'm crying again. These are happy tears. PATERNAL TEARS. Is that a thing? It's a thing now. I invented it."*
"Fenris is experiencing what he calls 'paternal tears,'" Sera reported.
"I'm experiencing them too." Kael's voice was rough. "What are we naming them?"
Sera looked down at her children. The silver-haired girl who would inherit her mother's Moon-Marked power. The black-haired boy who would inherit his father's Alpha strength.
"Luna," she said, touching her daughter's cheek. "After the Goddess who brought us together."
"And the boy?"
Kael studied his son — the golden eyes, the fierce grip, the tiny furrow between his brows that suggested he was already annoyed about something.
"Fen," he said. "Short for Fenris."

*"...wait. WHAT?! You're naming him after ME?! After the inner wolf? After the ridiculous, poetry-composing, emotional disaster of a wolf?! KAEL. KAEL, I'M — I don't even have WORDS. I've NEVER not had words. This is — this is the greatest honor of my ENTIRE EXISTENCE. I'm going to be the best inner-uncle in the history of inner-uncles. I'm going to teach him poetry. TERRIBLE poetry. The most terrible poetry in the WORLD."*
"He's touched," Sera translated. "He's going to teach Fen terrible poetry."
"Of course he is." Kael pressed a kiss to her forehead. "Our children. Luna and Fen."
"The Silver Queen's heir and the Alpha's heir."
"The future of the pack." He looked at his children with an expression of fierce, overwhelming love. "They're going to be incredible."
"They already are."
The pack celebrated the birth of the twins for three days.

Gifts poured in from every corner of the territory — blankets woven from the softest wool, toys carved from sacred oak, tiny silver pendants blessed by Magnus in the Moon Chamber. The servants — former servants now, equal members of the pack — organized a feast that put the bonding ceremony to shame.

Darius got drunk and announced that he would personally train both pups in combat "as soon as they can walk." Magnus declared that Luna would begin her Moon-Marked training at age five, "earlier if she shows signs of the Silver Fire." And the pack — the whole pack, nobles and warriors and former servants alike — howled their welcome to the new generation.

Sera stood at the window of the nursery on the third night, watching the full moon rise over the Blackwood. Luna and Fen were sleeping in their cradles, their tiny chests rising and falling in perfect synchronization.
"We did it," Kael said, coming to stand beside her. "Everything. The trial. The battle. The reforms. The pups. Everything the prophecy promised."
"Everything we promised each other." She leaned against him. "Are you happy?"
He was silent for a moment. When he spoke, his voice was quiet — not the cold Alpha voice, not the iron command voice, but something softer. Something that had taken him years to learn.

"I spent my whole life believing happiness was a weakness. That Alphas didn't get to be happy — they just got to be strong. Alone. Unbreakable." He pulled her closer. "And then you walked into my trial, and I heard Fenris screaming about chocolate and chew toys, and everything I thought I knew about strength just... crumbled."
"You learned that strength and happiness aren't opposites."

"I learned that you can't have one without the other. Not really. Not the way that matters." He pressed his lips to her hair. "So yes. I'm happy. For the first time in my life, I'm truly, completely, terrifyingly happy."
"Good." Sera smiled. "Because I'm happy too. And I plan to stay that way for a very long time."
"How long?"
She looked at her children — silver and black, moonlight and shadow, the future of everything they'd fought for.
"Forever," she said. "At least."
THE END
